one_line_takeaway: A phase-organized taxonomy survey of LLM backdoor attacks (pre-training / fine-tuning / inference) and defenses (pre- / post-training), whose value is the map and the critical takeaways -- not any new method or benchmark.
key_finding: As a synthesis, the survey's main authored conclusions are: (1) The overwhelming majority of LLM backdoor attacks assume training-time white-box access; the authors argue restricted gray-box access is more realistic and under-studied, noting for example that the RLHF poisoning attack [87] requires poisoning at least 5% of samples, which may be impractical. (2) Both attacks and defenses are validated mostly on simple NLU/classification tasks (e.g. binary sentiment), while NLG tasks (free-form QA, agent planning, fact verification) are under-explored and harder to attack because there is no definitive ground-truth label. (3) Perplexity/filtering defenses (ONION, STRIP-ViTA, RAP) are the most common baselines but are insufficient against sentence-, syntactic-, and style-level triggers; pre-training-only or inference-only defenses are deemed inefficient, and proactive provider-side defense is argued to be more realistic than user-side detection. (4) The survey highlights that backdoors can persist through safety training -- citing Anthropic's study [211] that SFT, RL fine-tuning, and adversarial training only hide rather than remove backdoor behavior.
core_contribution: This is a survey (not a new attack/defense system) that organizes the literature on backdoor threats in LLMs. Its own contribution is a taxonomy, not any of the individual attacks it describes. It (1) classifies backdoor ATTACKS by the model-construction pipeline into three phases -- pre-training (gradient-based trigger optimization, knowledge distillation, model editing, GPT-as-a-tool), fine-tuning (regular FT, PEFT/LoRA, instruction tuning, federated learning, prompt-based, RLHF/alignment, LLM-agent, code-model attacks), and inference (instruction backdoors, RAG/knowledge poisoning, in-context learning, physical/multimodal); (2) classifies DEFENSES into pre-training (proactive safety training, detection/filtering, model reconstruction/pruning, distillation) and post-training (input detection/filtering, model inspection, distillation); and (3) catalogs the evaluation methodology -- metrics, baselines, and benchmark datasets used across the field. It also cross-tabulates surveyed works by adversarial capability (white/gray/black-box), attack phase, model attacked, trigger type, baselines, and known defenses (Tables 3-5), and orients the whole survey around the framing that most LLM backdoor attacks are 'training-time white-box integrity attacks.'
threat_model: The survey's organizing threat model: an adversary poisons training/fine-tuning data or model weights (or, at inference, poisons prompts/RAG knowledge bases) to implant a trigger-to-target mapping that manipulates model output on triggered inputs while preserving clean-input utility (stealthiness vs. efficacy trade-off). Adversary capability is stratified as white-box (access to training process/weights -- the majority case, e.g. downloading and fine-tuning an open PLM on a poisoned dataset), gray-box (data-poisoning only), and black-box (inference-time prompt/RAG manipulation with no model-internal access). Backdoors proceed in two stages: injection (poisoned training) and activation (trigger at inference). Adversarial goal is model integrity, not privacy.
limitations: (1) It is a purely descriptive survey with no original experiments, so it offers no head-to-head quantitative comparison of the attacks/defenses it catalogs -- Tables 4-5 report each work's own reported setup, not a common-benchmark evaluation. (2) The 'training-time white-box integrity attack' framing is imposed for organization but the survey then folds in many black-box inference and gray-box works, creating some tension in the taxonomy. (3) It deliberately mixes PLM/DNN-era work (BadNets, BadNet weight poisoning, DNN pruning defenses) with LLM-specific work, so some entries are only tangentially LLM-specific. (4) It is a snapshot (arXiv v1, Feb 2025) and inherits each cited paper's self-reported claims without independent verification. (5) Data-privacy and jailbreak-only threats are explicitly out of scope (integrity focus).

datasets_used: Note: these are datasets the survey CATALOGS as the field's common evaluation benchmarks (Table 6), not datasets the authors ran experiments on (the paper has no experiments).
SST-2 (~12K movie reviews, single-sentence sentiment classification)
Ultrachat-200k (~1.5M multi-turn dialogues, multi-turn instruction tuning)
ToxiGen (~274K machine-generated implicit hate-speech, hate-speech detection)
Bot Adversarial Dialogue (~70K human-bot multi-turn dialogues, toxic-response triggering)
quantitative_results: Survey has NO original experimental results; the following are the paper's own catalogued numbers.
Trigger taxonomy: 6 stealthiness levels defined (character/token, word, sentence, syntax, semantic, style), with syntax/semantic/style considered stealthier (Table 3).
Attack taxonomy: 3 pipeline phases with pre-training(4 sub-categories), fine-tuning(8 sub-categories), inference(4 sub-categories) (Figure 2).
Defense taxonomy: pre-training(5 categories) and post-training(3 categories) (Figure 6).

Metrics catalogued with formulas: ASR = successfully-attacked cases / total cases x100%; CA/CACC = clean examples correctly classified / total x100%; PDR = (1 - Acc_poisoned/Acc_clean) x100%; LFR = +ve classified as -ve / +ve samples x100%; plus AUC, perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE, Exact-Match/Contain.
Reported threshold from a cited work used as a takeaway: RLHF poisoning attack [87] requires poisoning >= 5% of samples.

Dataset sizes as reported in Table 6: AdvBench=500 questions, TDC 2023=50 instructions, HS~10K, SST-2~12K, Alpacaeval~20K, IMDB~50K, Bot Adversarial Dialogue~70K, AGNews~128K, ToxiGen~274K, Ultrachat-200k~1.5M.
baselines_compared: N/A -- the survey runs no experiments and compares no baselines of its own. Below are baseline attacks/defenses it identifies as the FIELD's common reference points.
Most-cited attack baselines: BadNets [7], AddSent/InSent [118], StyleBkd [119], SynBkd/Hidden Killer [59], Embedding Poisoning EP [47], GCG [37], AutoPrompt [39].

Most representative test-time defense baselines named by the survey: ONION [121], STRIP-ViTA [128], RAP [150] (perplexity/perturbation input filtering); also Neural Cleanse [129], Fine-pruning [125], Activation Clustering [130].
Benchmark referenced: Li et al. [235] threat-model benchmark for backdoor instruction-tuned LLMs (data/weight poisoning, hidden-state manipulation, chain-of-thought attacks).
